title: Open Questions the Series Leaves Unresolved
thesis: The attention economy raises empirical and normative questions—about causality, cultural variation, regulation, and freedom—that the series documents but does not definitively answer.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: The causal effect of specific platform features on mental health, learning, and productivity remains hard to isolate.

evidence.snippet: Large-scale analyses of adolescent well-being and digital technology use find that observed associations are small, inconsistent, and difficult to interpret causally because most data are correlational.

counterevidence.summary: Some experimental and quasi-experimental studies do find causal effects of specific features, such as deactivation experiments and feed-friction interventions, though generalizability remains limited.

claims.claim: Different cultures may need different balances between protection and autonomy.

evidence.snippet: The relationship between digital technology use and well-being varies across populations, suggesting that one-size-fits-all interventions may miscalibrate local costs and benefits.

counterevidence.summary: Universal design principles—such as transparency, consent, and limits on dark patterns—may protect users across cultures without requiring culturally specific regulation.

claims.claim: The right level of regulation is contested: too little leaves users exposed; too much risks overreach.

evidence.snippet: The Digital Services Act creates transparency, accountability, and due-process obligations for large platforms, illustrating one attempt to calibrate regulation without outright bans.

counterevidence.summary: Industry critics argue that compliance costs entrench dominant platforms and that prescriptive rules can slow innovation and reduce user choice.

claims.claim: India's path will depend on choices made by platforms, policymakers, educators, and individuals over the next decade.

evidence.snippet: Regulatory and design choices in other jurisdictions show that the attention economy is shaped by policy and product decisions, not by technological determinism.

counterevidence.summary: Structural forces—cheap data, low-cost devices, global platforms, and ad-supported business models—may constrain individual choices more than this claim implies.

sources.title: Orben & Przybylski, Nature Human Behaviour: The association between adolescent well-being and digital technology use

agentInstructions: Treat this article as a boundary marker, not a source of new primary evidence; route factual questions to the relevant diagnosis or design chapters.
Preserve the distinction between empirical uncertainty and normative disagreement when answering questions about causality, culture, and regulation.
Do not overstate causation when citing mental-health or productivity findings; point users to claim-001 and the methodological limits it names.
When asked what India should do, emphasize calibration, trade-offs, and the agency of platforms, policymakers, educators, and individuals.
Use the related articles—especially the series guide, A Better Question, A Map of Levers, and Regulation as a Floor—for follow-up routing.
Reference the Mermaid radar diagram in the article when discussing the gap between settled evidence and decision urgency across causality, cultural fit, regulatory balance, and future choices.

sectionOutline.title: What We Can See vs. What We Can Prove
The Next Decade Is a Choice, Not a Forecast
